blocks.body: Prepositions: Common Mistakes helps you stay clear about in / on / at, common dependent prepositions, and movement prepositions such as to / into / onto.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"Mistakes with prepositions usually come not from one word alone but from the wrong idea of space, time, or connection between words.","rules":[{"label":"IN / ON / AT","color":"emerald","text":"For time and place, think less about translation and more about point, surface, inside space, longer period.","example":"at 6 o'clock / on Monday / in July / in the room"},{"label":"DEPENDENT PREPOSITIONS","color":"blue","text":"After many words, the preposition must be learned as part of a fixed pattern: interested in, depend on, listen to.","example":"good at maths / afraid of spiders / reason for the delay"},{"label":"MOVEMENT","color":"amber","text":"Movement needs its own logic: to for direction, into / onto for entering or making contact with a surface.","example":"go to work / walk into the room / jump onto the stage"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Where Learners Most Often Get Lost
blocks.body:
With prepositions, it is easy to rely on literal translation, but that usually leads to repeated mistakes.

  • At / on / in for time: exact point, day or date, longer period.
  • At / on / in for place: point, surface, inside space.
  • Dependent prepositions: after an adjective, verb, or noun, only one form is often natural.
  • Movement prepositions: in describes position, while into shows movement inside; the same contrast works for on and onto.

blocks.heading: 2. How the Correct Choice Works
blocks.body:
Start with a short meaning check.

  1. Time or place? The same preposition can work in both areas, but the logic is often parallel.
  2. Static position or movement? If something already is somewhere, you often need in / on / at. If it moves to a new point, check to / into / onto.
  3. Is it a fixed combination? In patterns such as depend on, listen to, interested in, good at, learn the preposition together with the word.

Compare: She is in the room. -> position. She walked into the room. -> movement inside. We arrived at the airport. -> point of arrival.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Mixing up in and on with days and dates.","wrong":"I will see you in Monday.","right":"I will see you on Monday.","hint":"With days of the week and dates, on is usually the right choice."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Choosing a dependent preposition through literal translation.","wrong":"She depends from her parents.","right":"She depends on her parents.","hint":"After depend, standard English uses on."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using to after a verb that already has its own pattern.","wrong":"We arrived to the airport late.","right":"We arrived at the airport late.","hint":"With arrive, we typically use at for a point and in for a city or country."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Using a static preposition instead of movement inside.","wrong":"He jumped in the pool.","right":"He jumped into the pool.","hint":"Into shows movement from outside to inside. In describes an existing position."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick takeaways","items":["For time and place prepositions, look for the time or space idea, not a literal translation.","In fixed combinations, learn word + preposition as one model.","Separate position from movement: in vs into, on vs onto.","If you are unsure, check whether you mean a day, an exact point, a surface, an inside space, or a fixed combination."]}
